Project Overview

A line-following robot built for the ECE 5 Lab 4 competition. The robot uses an array of photoresistors to detect a black line on a white surface, and a PID control loop to keep itself centered on the line, correcting motor speeds in real time based on the detected position relative to center.

Hardware overview — potentiometers set P/I/D gains and base speed without re-flashing; rubber bands on the rear wheels improve traction; the detached green board (right) is the photoresistor array inside its custom lightshield

Drag Race — 1st Place

The drag race prioritized raw straight-line speed. Running maximum motor output with a low proportional gain (P=40) kept corrections fast but minimal, since the track had few sharp turns.

Drag Race run — 7.96 sec

PID Control System

Error is computed as the actual line position minus the desired center. Three terms combine to correct motor speeds each control loop iteration:

P
Proportional
Corrections based on current error magnitude
I
Integral
Corrects steady-state error by accumulating past errors over time
D
Derivative
Predicts future error from rate of change, damping oscillation
Basic line following

Hardware: Early Build and Modifications

Initial assembly used a breadboard layout to prototype the circuit before committing to final wiring. A custom lightshield was later designed to block ambient light from the photoresistors, which was the single largest source of noise in the line-detection signal.

Early build stage — breadboard layout before chassis modifications. The final robot consolidated all wiring to reduce interference with the lightshield.

Tuning Strategy

Each event required different PID parameters because the optimal tradeoff between correction speed and accuracy changed with track geometry.

  • Drag Race — max speed (nominal 200, pot 60), P=40, I=100, D=100. Prioritized raw speed on a track with few sharp turns.
  • Frequency Sweep — slower speed (nominal 30, pot 100), P=100, I=100, D=100. Increasingly tight curves demanded aggressive correction at the expense of speed.
  • Circuit Loop — balanced (nominal 150, pot 70), P=95, I=100, D=100. Smooth, fast sustained laps.
Frequency Sweep — 1st Place, Checkpoint 9
Circuit Loop — 2nd Place, 34.68 loops

Chassis and CAD

The team modified the base chassis in CAD to improve reliability: motor mount clearances, board routing channels, and a close-fitting lightshield box that seats over the photoresistor array.

Updated chassis (left) with motor mounts and board clearance cutouts; lightshield (right) — a close-fitting enclosure that blocks ambient light from the photoresistor array

Competition Day

Late in the competition, the team identified that the ball wheel was inconsistently making contact with the track, alternately rolling and lifting, which introduced unpredictable speed changes. Taping it in a fixed position ensured consistent ground contact throughout each run.

Team Tachyon after the competition — Will Hovel, Andranik Maghakyan, Gage Schopen, Clinton Truong
Harder circuit loop

Key Takeaway

Predictability in the mechanical system is just as important as the control algorithm. A well-tuned PID controller still fails if the physical platform behaves inconsistently. The ball wheel fix was the most impactful single change on competition day.
